title: Skin in the Game
subtitle: Hidden Asymmetries in Daily Life
author: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
themes: Decision-makers must bear the consequences of their actions
Hidden asymmetries corrupt systems and concentrate risk on the vulnerable
A small intransigent minority can impose its will on the majority
The Silver Rule (do not do unto others) is more robust than the Golden Rule
Privatized gains and socialized losses (the Bob Rubin trade) is the root of systemic fragility
True rationality is defined by survival, not abstract optimization
Time is the only reliable filter for knowledge and ethics
awards: Final volume of the Incerto series
excerpt: The principle that those who make decisions must bear the consequences of those decisions is the oldest and most fundamental rule of justice — from Hammurabi's Code to modern risk management. When decision-makers are insulated from the downside of their actions, they transfer risk to the vulnerable, corrupt the system, and eventually destroy the very institutions that protect them. Skin in the game is not just a moral imperative; it is the only reliable mechanism for filtering competence from charlatanism and ensuring that complex systems survive.
